sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 520, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at [8-11, 60-62] with white on right. Dropped item at [4-7, 60-63] directly above player. Player is adjacent to dropped item from below!
- Plan: Try pressing SPACE to collect the dropped item. If it works, then try to deposit it in the bar.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 519 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 7.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The SPACE action didn't change the board (board_changed: false). So pressing SPACE when adjacent to the dropped item doesn't collect it. This means dropped items cannot be recollected.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. The only time SPACE worked was when I collected target 3 initially. After that, SPACE never worked again, even when the player was adjacent to targets.
Wait, let me re-examine the initial collection. The player was at [48-51, 32-35], moved UP 8 to [40-43, 32-35], and pressed SPACE. Target 3 was at [36-39, 32-35]. The player was adjacent from below (player top at row 40, target bottom at row 39). After SPACE, target 3 was collected.
But after that, when I tried to collect target 1 or 2, SPACE didn't work. Why?
One possibility: the player can only collect one target per level. After collecting target 3, the player's white frame expanded, and subsequent SPACE actions don't collect more targets.
Another possibility: the player needs to be in a specific orientation to collect targets. When I collected target 3, the white frame was on top (facing the target). When I tried to collect target 1 or 2, the white frame might have been on a different side.
Let me check the player's orientation when collecting target 3. The player moved UP 8, so the white frame was on top. After collecting, the white frame expanded to 4x4.
When I tried to collect target 1, the player moved UP 8 and RIGHT 8. After UP 8, the white frame was on top. After RIGHT 8, the white frame moved to the right. So when pressing SPACE, the white frame was on the right, not facing target 1.
Maybe the player needs to face the target (white frame on the side facing the target) to collect it!
Let me test this hypothesis. Target 2 is at [28-31, 16-19]. To collect from below, the player needs to be at [32-35, 16-19] with white frame on top. To collect from the right, the player needs to be at [28-31, 20-23] with white frame on the left.
Currently, the player is at [8-11, 60-62] with white frame on the right. Let me try to approach target 2 from the right, with white frame on the left.
To get white frame on the left, I need to move LEFT. So I should approach target 2 from the right by moving LEFT.
Player at [8-11, 60-62], target 2 at [28-31, 16-19].
To be adjacent from right: player at [28-31, 20-23] with white frame on left.
- DOWN: 28-8 = 20 rows
- LEFT: 60-23 = 37 cols (or RIGHT 27 cols)
But if I move DOWN first, the white frame moves to the bottom. Then if I move LEFT, the white frame moves to the left. So the final orientation would be white on left, which is what I want!
Let me try: DOWN 20, LEFT 37, SPACE.
Wait, but the player is 3 rows tall (with white on right). When moving DOWN, the white frame moves to the bottom, making the player 4 rows tall. When moving LEFT, the white frame moves to the left, making the player 3 rows tall and 4 cols wide.
So after DOWN 20: player at [8+20, 60-62] = [28, 60-62] for green, white at [31, 60-62]. Player is 4 rows tall: [28-31, 60-62].
After LEFT 37: player at [28-31, 60-37] = [28-31, 23] for green, white at [28-31, 22]. Player is 3 rows tall: [28-30, 22-24] with white on left.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just try it and see.
Actually, I realize I've been spending way too many actions on this. Let me try a completely different approach.
Maybe the goal is NOT to collect targets at all. Maybe the goal is to do something else with the bar.
Let me look at the bar more carefully. The bar is at [28-31, 28-39] with blue frame and gray interior [29-30, 29-38]. The gray interior is 2x10 = 20 cells.
What if the goal is to fill the bar's interior with blue squares? The targets have blue squares, and maybe the player needs to "shoot" or "transfer" the blue squares into the bar.
But how? The player can move and press SPACE. Maybe pressing SPACE when adjacent to the bar (not inside) deposits the held item into the bar.
Let me try this. First, I need to collect a target (which I already did for target 3, but the item dropped). Then I need to move to the bar's edge and press SPACE.
But the item dropped when I moved, so I don't have anything to deposit.
OK, let me try a completely different hypothesis. Maybe the game is about matching colors or patterns. The targets have blue squares, and the bar has a gray interior. Maybe the goal is to change the bar's interior color from gray to blue.
But how? The player can only move and press SPACE.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial collection. When I collected target 3, the player's white frame expanded to 4x4 and the blue square appeared inside. Then when I moved, the white frame shrunk and the blue square dropped as a separate entity.
What if the goal is to collect all 3 targets and then the level completes? But the item drops when I move, so I can't collect multiple targets.
Unless... the player can collect multiple targets without moving? That doesn't make sense because the targets are in different locations.
OK, I'm going to try a completely different approach. Let me reset my understanding and look at the game from scratch.
The game has:
1. A player (green body + white frame)
2. Three targets (charcoal frames with blue squares)
3. A bar (blue frame with gray interior)
4. A timer (pink bar at bottom)
The player starts at the bottom, below the bar. The targets are around the bar. The bar's interior is a death zone.
What if the goal is to navigate the player to a specific location? Or to collect all 3 targets in a specific order?
Let me try to collect target 3 again, but this time without moving after collecting. Target 3 is no longer at [36-39, 32-35] because it was collected. The dropped item is at [4-7, 60-63].
Wait, maybe the dropped item IS target 3, just relocated. And maybe I need to collect it again to "reactivate" it, then move it to the bar.
But pressing SPACE when adjacent to the dropped item didn't work.
OK, I'm stuck. Let me try one more thing: approach target 2 from below with white frame on top, and press SPACE.
Player at [8-11, 60-62] with white on right. To get white on top, I need to move UP. But the player is already near the top (row 8).
Let me try: DOWN 24, LEFT 44, SPACE. This should move the player to [32-35, 16-19] (from below target 2) with white on top.
Actually, I realize I need to be more careful about the player's orientation. Let me trace through the movements:
- Player at [8-11, 60-62] with white on right (3 rows tall, 4 cols wide).
- DOWN 1: white moves to bottom, player is 4 rows tall. Player at [8-11, 60-62] → [9-12, 60-62].
- DOWN 24: player at [8+24-1, 60-62] = [31, 60-62] for green, white at [34, 60-62]. Player is 4 rows tall: [31-34, 60-62].
- LEFT 1: white moves to left, player is 3 rows tall. Player at [31-33, 59-61] with white at [31-33, 58].
- LEFT 44: player at [31-33, 59-44, 61-44] = [31-33, 15, 17] with white at [31-33, 14]. Player is 3 rows tall: [31-33, 14-17].
Target 2 is at [28-31, 16-19]. The player at [31-33, 14-17] is adjacent to target 2 from below-left. The player's top is at row 31, target 2's bottom is at row 31. So they share an edge at row 31. The player's right is at col 17, target 2's left is at col 16. So they share an edge at col 16-17.
Wait, the player at [31-33, 14-17] and target 2 at [28-31, 16-19]:
- Player's top: row 31
- Target 2's bottom: row 31
- They share row 31, but the player's top edge is at row 31 and the target's bottom edge is at row 31. So they are adjacent vertically at row 31.
- Player's cols: 14-17
- Target 2's cols: 16-19
- Overlap in cols: 16-17
So the player and target 2 share an edge at rows 31, cols 16-17. This means they are adjacent!
And the white frame is on the left (from moving LEFT), which is not facing target 2 (which is above-right of the player).
Hmm, maybe the white frame needs to face the target. Let me try a different approach: move DOWN first, then LEFT, so the white frame ends up on the left. But target 2 is above the player, so the white frame should be on top.
Let me try: LEFT 44, DOWN 24, SPACE. This should move the player to [8-11, 16-19] after LEFT 44, then to [32-35, 16-19] after DOWN 24. The white frame would be on the bottom (from moving DOWN), which is not facing target 2 (which is above).
Actually, I think the white frame orientation doesn't matter for collection. Let me just try DOWN 24, LEFT 44, SPACE and see what happens.
Wait, I already tried something similar before (UP 28, RIGHT 20, SPACE) and it didn't work. The player ended up at [8-11, 60-62], not near target 2.
I think the issue is that my position calculations are wrong. The player's movement is affected by the white frame orientation, and I'm not accounting for it correctly.
Let me try a simpler approach: just move the player to a known position and check. For example, move LEFT until the player is at col 16-19, then move DOWN until the player is at row 32-35, then press SPACE.
But this would take many actions, and I've already used 519 actions.
Actually, let me try something completely different. What if the goal is to press SPACE while the player is at a specific location (like the bar's edge)? Let me try moving to the bar's edge and pressing SPACE.
The bar is at [28-31, 28-39]. The player is at [8-11, 60-62]. To get to the bar's edge (say, the left edge at col 27), I need to move LEFT 60-27 = 33 cols. To get to row 28-31, I need to move DOWN 28-8 = 20 rows.
Let me try: DOWN 20, LEFT 33, SPACE. This should move the player to [28-31, 27-30] (left of the bar), then press SPACE.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: SPACE when adjacent to dropped item did nothing (board_changed: false). Dropped items cannot be recollected. Only target 3 was ever collected (step 412). After that, SPACE never collected anything.
Key question: Why did SPACE work for target 3 but not for targets 1, 2, or the dropped item?
Hypothesis: Maybe the white frame must face the target. When collecting target 3, white frame was on top (facing target above). For targets 1 and 2, white frame was on wrong side.
New plan: Try collecting target 2 from below with white frame on top. Move LEFT first (white→left), then DOWN (white→bottom)... no, need white on top. Move RIGHT first (white→right), then DOWN (white→bottom)... still not top.
Actually: to get white on top, last movement must be UP. So approach from below by moving UP last.
Let me try: LEFT to align cols, then UP to approach from below with white on top.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at [8-11, 60-62], target 2 at [28-31, 16-19]
# To collect from below with white on top:
# Player needs to be at [32-35, 16-19] with white on top
# Last move must be UP to get white on top
# So: move to [33-36, 16-19] first, then UP 1
# From [8-11, 60-62]:
# LEFT 44 → [8-11, 16-19] (white on left)
# DOWN 24 → [32-35, 16-19] (white on bottom)
# UP 1 → [31-34, 16-19] (white on top)
# But this is inefficient. Let me just try LEFT 44, DOWN 23, UP 1, SPACE
# Actually, let me try a simpler sequence: LEFT 44, DOWN 20, SPACE
# After LEFT 44: player at [8-11, 16-19] with white on left
# After DOWN 20: player at [32-35, 16-19] with white on bottom
# Target 2 at [28-31, 16-19], player at [32-35, 16-19]
# Player top at row 32, target bottom at row 31 → adjacent!
action(['LEFT']*44 + ['DOWN']*20 + ['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Let me try: LEFT to align cols, then UP to approach from below with white on top.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at [8-11, 60-62], target 2 at [28-31, 16-19]
# To collect from below with white on top:
# Player needs to be at [32-35, 16-19] with white on top
# Last move must be UP to get white on top
# So: move to [33-36, 16-19] first, then UP 1
# From [8-11, 60-62]:
# LEFT 44 → [8-11, 16-19] (white on left)
# DOWN 24 → [32-35, 16-19] (white on bottom)
# UP 1 → [31-34, 16-19] (white on top)
# But this is inefficient. Let me just try LEFT 44, DOWN 23, UP 1, SPACE
# Actually, let me try a simpler sequence: LEFT 44, DOWN 20, SPACE
# After LEFT 44: player at [8-11, 16-19] with white on left
# After DOWN 20: player at [32-35, 16-19] with white on bottom
# Target 2 at [28-31, 16-19], player at [32-35, 16-19]
# Player top at row 32, target bottom at row 31 → adjacent!
action(['LEFT']*44 + ['DOWN']*20 + ['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
